events.text: Patient is a 68-year-old African American male, height 5'11", weight 210 lbs, BMI 29.3, former smoker with 10 pack-year history, family history significant for prostate cancer in father diagnosed at age 62.
At age 62, patient was diagnosed with prostate adenocarcinoma (ICD-O site C61.9, histology 8140/3) following evaluation for elevated PSA of 12.4 ng/mL detected on routine screening.

Transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy at age 62 revealed adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 4+4=8 (Grade Group 4), involving 6 of 12 cores, with perineural invasion identified; no lymphovascular invasion seen.
At age 62, staging workup including CT abdomen/pelvis and technetium-99m bone scan showed no evidence of distant metastatic disease; clinical stage T2cN0M0.
At age 62, patient underwent robot-assisted radical prostatectomy with bilateral pelvic lymph node dissection; pathologic stage pT3aN0M0 with positive surgical margins at the apex.

Radical prostatectomy specimen at age 62 showed Gleason 4+4=8 adenocarcinoma with extraprostatic extension, negative lymph nodes (0/18), positive apical margin, and seminal vesicle invasion was absent.
At age 63, patient received adjuvant intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) to the prostate bed, total dose 66 Gy in 33 fractions, due to positive surgical margins.
At age 63, post-radiation PSA nadir was 0.04 ng/mL; patient was placed on active surveillance with PSA monitoring every 3 months.
At age 65, PSA rose to 0.8 ng/mL consistent with biochemical recurrence; testosterone was 420 ng/dL.
At age 65, patient was started on androgen deprivation therapy with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg intramuscular injection every 3 months for biochemical recurrence.
At age 65, after 3 months of ADT, PSA decreased to 0.2 ng/mL and serum testosterone was suppressed to 18 ng/dL.
At age 67, technetium-99m bone scan revealed new uptake in the L3 vertebral body and right posterior iliac crest, consistent with osseous metastatic disease.
At age 67, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis showed sclerotic lesions in L3 and right ilium consistent with bone metastases; no visceral metastatic disease identified.
At age 67, PSA had risen to 8.6 ng/mL while on leuprolide with serum testosterone of 12 ng/dL, meeting criteria for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC).
At age 67, disease was reclassified as metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), stage T3aN0M1b, with bone metastases to lumbar spine and pelvis while on continuous ADT.
At age 67, enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily was initiated for mCRPC in addition to continued leuprolide; baseline PSA was 8.6 ng/mL.
At age 67, patient developed grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 hypertension (BP 148/92 mmHg) attributed to enzalutamide; antihypertensive medication amlodipine 5 mg was added.
At age 67, after 3 months on enzalutamide, PSA declined to 2.1 ng/mL representing a 76% decline from baseline; bone pain improved.

At age 67, next-generation sequencing (FoundationOne CDx) performed on archival prostatectomy tissue revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 c.5946delT frameshift mutation, PTEN loss (homozygous deletion), and TMPRSS2-ERG fusion; tumor mutational burden was 4 mutations/Mb; microsatellite stable.
At age 68, PSMA PET/CT showed persistent PSMA-avid lesions in L3 and right ilium with a new focus of uptake in the T10 vertebral body; no visceral disease.
At age 68, PSA rose to 12.4 ng/mL on enzalutamide indicating disease progression; enzalutamide was discontinued.
At age 68, patient was transitioned to olaparib 300 mg orally twice daily given the presence of pathogenic BRCA2 mutation, in addition to continued leuprolide.
At age 68, patient experienced grade 2 anemia (hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL) and grade 1 nausea while on olaparib; erythropoietin-stimulating agent was initiated.
At age 69, PSA declined to 4.2 ng/mL on olaparib, representing a 66% decline; patient reported improvement in bone pain.
At age 69, restaging CT and bone scan showed stable osseous disease with no new metastatic lesions while on olaparib therapy.
At age 70, patient sustained a pathologic fracture of the right femoral neck related to bone metastatic disease; orthopedic consultation obtained.
At age 70, patient underwent right hip hemiarthroplasty for pathologic fracture of the femoral neck secondary to metastatic prostate cancer.
At age 70, palliative radiation therapy was delivered to the right proximal femur, 30 Gy in 10 fractions, for pain control and local disease management following surgical fixation.
At age 70, PSA rose to 18.7 ng/mL on olaparib indicating further disease progression; olaparib was discontinued after approximately 2 years of therapy.
At age 70, docetaxel 75 mg/m2 intravenously every 3 weeks with prednisone 5 mg twice daily was initiated as next-line therapy for progressive mCRPC.
At age 70, after cycle 3 of docetaxel, patient developed grade 3 neutropenia (ANC 800/uL) and grade 2 peripheral neuropathy; docetaxel dose was reduced to 60 mg/m2.

PATHOLOGY REPORT

DIAGNOSIS AND FINDINGS:
Transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy at age 62 revealed adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 4+4=8 (Grade Group 4), involving 6 of 12 cores, with perineural invasion identified; no lymphovascular invasion seen.

CLINICAL PROGRESS NOTE

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
At age 62, staging workup including CT abdomen/pelvis and technetium-99m bone scan showed no evidence of distant metastatic disease; clinical stage T2cN0M0.

The patient was seen in clinic today for ongoing oncologic management.

scenario_blurb: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). Rising PSA on ADT. Treated with enzalutamide, abiraterone, docetaxel, or cabazitaxel. Include BRCA1/2 and homologous recombination deficiency testing. Some with bone-predominant and some with visceral metastases.
